mnemonics.title: Main Models Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: D-MAC-RAB: 'Different Mandatory Access Rules Build Absolute Control' - Remember the four main models: DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC

mnemonics.title: DAC Memory Hook
mnemonics.content: DAC = 'Data Access Controlled by Owner' - The owner has discretion to grant or revoke access

mnemonics.title: MAC Memory Hook
mnemonics.content: MAC = 'Mandatory Adherence to Classifications' - System enforces security labels, users cannot change access rules

mnemonics.title: RBAC Memory Hook
mnemonics.content: RBAC = 'Rights Based on Assigned Roles' - Access determined by job function/role, not individual identity

mnemonics.title: ABAC Memory Hook
mnemonics.content: ABAC = 'Access By Attribute Comparison' - Decisions based on user, resource, action, and environment attributes

acronyms.usage: Use when owner controls who accesses their resources (like file permissions in OS)
Use for high-security systems where government/classifications dictate access (military, classified data)
Use in organizations where access matches job responsibilities (employee, manager, admin roles)
Use when access depends on multiple dynamic attributes (time, location, department, security clearance)

visualTips.visualization: Picture a private house with the owner holding keys - they decide who enters each room. The owner has complete discretion.
Imagine a military base with security checkpoints - everyone must follow strict classification levels (Top Secret > Secret > Confidential > Unclassified). No one can override these rules.
Think of a company org chart with job titles (Manager, Employee, Intern) - each title has specific doors it can open, regardless of which person holds that role.
Picture a smart building with sensors checking multiple things at once: your ID card (who), the room (what), the time (when), and your purpose (why) - all factors combined determine entry.
Think of a pyramid: MAC at top (most restrictive), then RBAC, then ABAC, then DAC at bottom (most flexible). Moving up = more control but less flexibility.

analogies.analogy: A personal diary - the owner (you) decide who can read it. You can share with friends, family, or keep it private. You control the access completely.
A bank vault - strict security levels determine access. Only people with proper clearance (like 'Manager' level) can enter certain areas, regardless of who asks. Rules are non-negotiable.

A hotel key card system - a housekeeping staff card opens all guest rooms but not the front desk; a front desk card opens guest rooms and front desk but not the storage. Access is tied to the role, not the individual person.

Airport security boarding - you need correct ticket class (attribute), valid ID (attribute), matching flight (attribute), and must pass security within certain time (environment attribute). Multiple attributes determine access.
RBAC is like a club membership - all members get the same benefits. ABAC is like Netflix recommendations - what you see depends on your watch history, location, device, and preferences.
